mechanism: Agents repeatedly participate in a public goods game where contributions generate shared benefits minus costs. After observing aggregate contributions (imperfectly in treatment), agents choose whether to incur cost to punish others. The study measures how punishment availability and severity affect equilibrium contribution levels and net payoffs across multiple rounds. This involves comparing outcome distributions under different punishment regimes to infer motivational responses and coordination on equilibria.

In a delegation game, one agent (principal) assigns decision authority to another (subordinate) and chooses effort level; the subordinate then chooses effort given their incentive structure. Outcomes depend on joint effort via a payoff function. The experiment measures whether retaining authority despite delegation being material-optimal reveals nonpecuniary utility, and whether authority triggers effort distortions relative to optimal play.

The selection of an arbitrator is modeled as an implementation problem: two conflicting parties participate in a procedure to choose the arbitrator who will rule their dispute. The study derives theoretical predictions about which selection mechanisms (veto-rank, shortlisting) satisfy desirable properties (truthfulness, efficiency, robustness to coalitions) and experimentally tests whether subjects' choices match theoretical equilibrium predictions under each mechanism.

Agents play a dynamic matching model where they repeatedly encounter a trading partner and must decide whether to exchange goods (with money or via gift exchange). The model has multiple equilibria; subjects' choices reveal which equilibrium they coordinate on. Comparisons between money and no-money conditions show whether money serves as a coordination device and which institutional setup yields higher welfare.

Households make treatment-seeking decisions for a health condition when diagnostic information (test results) and subsidized antimicrobial drugs are available at varying prices. The outcome is a binary treatment decision per case. The experiment varies subsidy level and test availability to quantify the trade-off between access and overtreatment, measuring outcomes as rates of correct vs. incorrect treatment decisions at the household level.

Female MBA students make educational and career decisions (exam performance, class participation, salary expectations, work preferences) which are sometimes observable by peers and sometimes private. The experiment measures whether observable performance differs from private performance, and isolates the effect of visibility by single male peer observations. Comparisons across observability conditions and demographic groups identify causal effects of reputational concerns on choices.
domain: behavioral economics, gender and labor markets

mechanism: Individuals are exposed to international information and cultural beliefs through migration to destinations with varying institutional properties. Electoral outcomes and political preferences are measured at community and individual levels before and after a migration wave. Causal identification uses the quasi-experimental variation in migration rates across communities and destinations to estimate the effect of information/cultural exposure on political outcomes.

An 18-item psychometric scale is developed to measure receptiveness to opposing views. Factor analysis extracts latent dimensions. Validity is assessed via convergent (correlations with related constructs) and discriminant (low correlations with unrelated constructs) validity. Criterion validity is established by correlating scale scores with behavioral outcomes: information consumption, attention during disagreement, argument evaluation impartiality, and news consumption patterns.

Longitudinal panel regression analysis of organizational units over time relates international resource access (predictor) to product market internationalization (mediator) to financial performance (outcome), with moderation by time period and organizational group membership. Fixed-effects or random-effects panel estimators are used to identify effects while controlling for unobserved unit-specific heterogeneity, yielding conditional point estimates of association strengths.

Participants estimate calorie content of food images shown in isolation or alongside reference foods. The dependent variable is the calorie estimate (continuous). The independent variable is presence/absence of a reference food. Potential mechanisms are tested via comparison of estimates across conditions to assess whether estimates are averaged (averaging hypothesis) or anchored to a reference value (anchoring hypothesis). Mediation by food healthiness ratings is tested.

A Bayesian probabilistic model of pragmatic language understanding is fitted to individual-level choices in a reference game. Latent depth-of-reasoning parameters vary across individuals. Bayesian model comparison tests whether a homogeneous population model (single depth parameter) or heterogeneous population model (varying depth per individual) better predicts observed choices. Model fit is assessed via marginal likelihood or information criteria.

Participants are given opportunities to act dishonestly for monetary gain (e.g., reporting false performance to earn payment). The outcome is the degree to which they lie. The experiments test two mechanisms enabling self-concept maintenance under dishonesty: inattention to moral standards (via cognitive load or moral disengagement) and categorization malleability (redefining what counts as dishonesty). Observed behavior reveals how much dishonesty subjects permit themselves.

Shoppers evaluate products displayed on tables with surfaces of different materials (surface properties of context). Self-view (independent vs. interdependent) is manipulated or measured. Product evaluation is the outcome. The mechanism predicts that independent self-view triggers analytic processing, producing contrast effects (negative association between table association and product evaluation), while interdependent self-view triggers holistic processing, producing assimilation effects.

Consumer preference for counterfeit brands is modeled as a function of social-motivational functions of the attitude (social-adjustive vs. value-expressive). Moral beliefs are a second predictor. The outcome is preference for counterfeit vs. authentic products. Moderation analysis tests whether moral beliefs affect counterfeit preference only under value-expressive motivation. Marketing-mix elements are manipulated to experimentally alter which function is activated.

Consumer attitudes toward products are characterized by latent functions (utilitarian vs. symbolic). Branding is an experimental manipulation. The outcome is the function-loading of attitudes: which functional dimension is activated. A second outcome is persuasiveness of appeal types (utilitarian vs. symbolic). The analysis tests moderation: whether the function-matching advantage (utilitarian appeals persuade utilitarian-attitude consumers) holds at brand level as well as category level.

Decision delegation (outsourcing choice to a surrogate) is predicted to deplete self-regulatory resources more than independent decision-making, even though delegation requires less effort, because it triggers ego-threat. Resource depletion is measured as impaired self-control in a subsequent task. Moderation by free-will affirmation and mediation by memory of delegated choices are tested across multiple experiments. Comparisons use depletion-task performance as the outcome.

Consumers state their willingness to pay (WTP) or willingness to accept (WTA) for a risky prospect under different linguistic frames (lottery, raffle, coin flip, gamble vs. gift certificate, voucher). The outcome variables are WTP and WTA (continuous). The mechanism tests whether frame-induced risk salience shifts focus (buyers focus on worst outcomes and risk; sellers focus on best and worst outcomes, not frame). Mediation and moderation are tested experimentally.

Static logo images are presented and perceived movement is measured (via self-report and eye-tracking metrics). The outcome is consumer engagement (self-report and gaze patterns). A second outcome is brand attitude. Path analysis tests mediation: perceived movement → engagement → brand attitude. Moderation by congruence between movement perception and brand characteristics is assessed. Eye-tracking provides continuous gaze metrics; engagement and attitude are scalar ratings.

Scarcity salience (abundance vs. scarcity framing) is manipulated. Choice share of favorite vs. non-favorite items in a product class is the outcome. The mechanism predicts scarcity increases arousal; arousal polarizes item evaluations (favorite liked more, non-favorite liked less); polarization increases favorite choice share. Moderated mediation is tested: arousal is the mediator; experimentally induced arousal state is a moderator. Behavioral outcomes are choice shares; subjective outcomes are arousal ratings.

Consumers report the number of goals, level of perceived conflict between goals, stress/anxiety, and time pressure (subjective time scarcity). The analysis tests mediation: goal conflict → stress/anxiety → perceived time pressure, across multiple goal domains and types of conflict. Interventions (slow breathing, anxiety reappraisal) are tested for moderation of the conflict-to-time-pressure path. Behavioral outcomes include time allocation and willingness to pay for time-saving goods (continuous measures).
